title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — the consistent daily pattern is very helpful.

To narrow down where the leak is coming from: can you tell me what your deployment looks like? Specifically:

- Is the 4GB memory growth on the **TaskFlow application process itself**, or is that the total server memory (which might include a database, Redis, background workers, etc.)?
- If you have per-process metrics, which process is consuming the most memory by end of day?

This will help us figure out whether we're looking at an application-level leak, a database/cache issue, or something else entirely.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question. The 4GB is specifically the TaskFlow application process — I've been tracking it separately in Grafana. The database (PostgreSQL) and Redis both stay pretty stable throughout the day. It's definitely the main TaskFlow server process that's eating all the memory. The VM has 8GB total, so when TaskFlow hits 4GB+ things really start to degrade.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for confirming it's the TaskFlow process itself — that's a really useful data point.

Next, I want to understand what's driving the growth. A few specifics:

- **Which version of TaskFlow are you running?** And has this always happened, or did it start after a particular upgrade?
- **Does the memory growth correlate with user activity?** For example, does it climb faster during peak hours, or does it grow at a steady rate even overnight/weekends when fewer people are active?
- **Are you using any features that maintain long-lived connections** — such as real-time updates, WebSocket-based notifications, or live dashboards that users leave open all day?

The answer to whether growth is activity-driven vs. time-driven will significantly narrow down where to look.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
We're on TaskFlow v2.3 — we upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, and actually now that you mention it, that's right when this started. I didn't connect the two at first because the upgrade seemed to go smoothly and everything worked fine initially, but the timing lines up perfectly.

The memory growth definitely correlates with user activity. It climbs much faster during business hours when everyone is active, and it basically flattens out overnight and on weekends. On Monday mornings after a weekend restart, the server is nice and snappy.

Regarding long-lived connections — yes, v2.3 introduced "improved real-time notifications" which was actually one of the reasons we upgraded. Users get push notifications for task updates, mentions, etc. I'm not sure how many users leave those connections open all day, but most of our team keeps TaskFlow open in a browser tab throughout the workday.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
The timing lines up very well — the v2.3 upgrade introducing real-time notifications, combined with activity-correlated memory growth and users keeping TaskFlow open all day, strongly suggests the new notification system is the culprit.

To confirm this theory, could you check one thing for me? On the server, can you look at the **number of active WebSocket (or SSE) connections** at different points during the day? You can usually get this from:

- `ss -s` or `ss -t state established | grep <taskflow-port> | wc -l` on the server
- Or if TaskFlow has an admin/metrics endpoint (e.g., `/admin/stats` or Prometheus metrics), it may report active connection counts

Specifically: does the connection count **grow throughout the day** (suggesting connections aren't being closed when users navigate away or close tabs), or does it stay roughly proportional to the number of users currently online? This will tell us whether we're looking at a connection leak vs. a per-connection memory leak in the notification handlers.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I ran `ss -t state established | grep :8080 | wc -l` a few times today. The connection count actually stays pretty stable — it hovers around 180-200 during business hours, which is roughly in line with our active user count. It doesn't grow throughout the day the way memory does. When people go home, the connections drop off as expected.

So it doesn't look like connections themselves are leaking. The memory is growing even though the number of connections stays flat. That's interesting — does that mean something is accumulating *per request* rather than per connection? Our users are pretty active, probably averaging around 25 API requests per user per hour during the workday.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Great observation — the stable connection count combined with activity-correlated memory growth strongly suggests something is accumulating **per request or per event** inside the notification subsystem, rather than per connection. Think: an in-memory notification history, event replay buffer, or listener registry that grows with every task update but never gets pruned.

To confirm this theory, can you try one thing? **Disable the real-time notification feature** (if TaskFlow has a config flag for it, or by blocking the notification endpoint at a reverse proxy level) and observe whether the memory growth stops or dramatically slows.

If v2.3 doesn't have a feature toggle for notifications, an alternative test: do you see the same memory growth on a **low-activity test instance** with just 1-2 users — or does it only manifest at your scale of ~200 users × ~25 requests/hour? That would help confirm whether the leak is proportional to request volume.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I don't have a separate test instance set up right now, but I can check on the config flag. I looked through our TaskFlow config and there is a `ENABLE_REALTIME_NOTIFICATIONS` setting that was added in v2.3. I just turned it off about an hour ago and the memory has been completely flat since — it's sitting right where it was when I disabled it. So that pretty much confirms it's the notification system causing the leak.

Given what you said about something accumulating per request, that makes sense with what I'm seeing. With ~200 users doing ~25 requests/hour, that's around 5,000 requests per hour, and the memory growth does seem roughly proportional to that. Is there a known fix for this in a newer version, or should I just keep notifications disabled for now?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system causes progressive server degradation","problem":"The TaskFlow application process leaks memory proportional to request/event volume when the real-time notification feature (introduced in v2.3) is enabled. Memory grows from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over a business day with ~200 active users (~5,000 requests/hour), causing page loads >10s and API timeouts. Requires daily restart.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 real-time notification subsystem accumulates data per request or per notification event in an unbounded in-memory structure (e.g., notification history buffer, event replay queue, or listener/callback registry) that is never pruned or garbage collected. WebSocket/SSE connections themselves are managed correctly (stable count ~180-200), so the leak is in the per-event processing path, not connection lifecycle.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 with ENABLE_REALTIME_NOTIFICATIONS=true","Have multiple users (200 in reporter's case, but likely reproducible at smaller scale with proportionally longer observation) actively use the application, generating task updates and API requests","Monitor the TaskFlow application process memory over several hours","Observe memory climbing proportionally to request volume without recovery","Disable ENABLE_REALTIME_NOTIFICATIONS and observe memory stabilizes immediately"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2), PostgreSQL database, Redis, 8GB VM, ~200 active users, ~25 API requests/user/hour during business hours","severity":"high","impact":"All users on self-hosted v2.3 instances with real-time notifications enabled experience progressive degradation throughout the day, eventually rendering the application unusable. Workaround exists (disable notifications via ENABLE_REALTIME_NOTIFICATIONS=false), but sacrifices a key v2.3 feature.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the v2.3 real-time notification code path for unbounded in-memory data structures. Likely candidates: (1) an in-memory notification event log or replay buffer that appends per event but never evicts, (2) per-request callback/listener registrations that accumulate without cleanup, (3) a notification fan-out structure that caches events for delivery but never expires them. Look at what changed between v2.2 and v2.3 in the notification module. Add bounds (max size, TTL-based eviction, or periodic pruning) to whatever structure is growing.","proposed_test_case":"Create a load test that simulates sustained API request volume (e.g., 1,000 requests/minute) with real-time notifications enabled. Monitor application process memory (RSS) over a 1-hour period. Assert that memory usage stabilizes (reaches a steady state) rather than growing linearly with cumulative request count. Compare against the same test with notifications disabled as a control.","information_gaps":["Exact internal data structure causing the accumulation (requires code inspection of v2.3 notification module)","Whether a fix exists in a newer TaskFlow version (reporter asked but this is outside triage scope)","Minimum user/request threshold to trigger noticeable growth"]}
